text: The surgeon unfolds peritoneal attachments in the pelvis to assess redundancy, using a transverse port orientation with two left-hand and one right-hand robotic instruments. The sigmoid mesentery is tented up, and the peritoneal incision is made higher than usual due to the unusual coverage of the sacral promontory by iliac arteries and veins. The incision is carefully extended down towards the sacrum, exposing the iliac artery and vein, and revealing the periosteum.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to unfold peritoneal attachments in the pelvis, examining the redundancy of the tissue. The transverse port orientation is visible, with two instruments on the left and one on the right. The surgeon attempts to tent up the sigmoid mesentery.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision higher than usual due to the unusual positioning of the iliac arteries and veins over the sacral promontory. The incision is then extended down towards the sacrum, carefully exposing the area. An iliac artery is visible, with a vein positioned to the left, as the surgeon navigates into the landing zone, revealing some periosteum.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection, focusing on the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, which appears redundant and fibrotic due to chronic prolapse. Presacral veins are visible, and the surgeon carefully avoids them by staying on the yellow fat. The dissection follows the curve of the coccyx, revealing the levator muscle, before moving to the right-sided dissection to join the posterior plane.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection in the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, noting the absence of an extraperitoneal rectovaginal septum due to the patient's previous hysterectomy and low cul-de-sac.

The surgeon navigates a fibrotic and redundant plane in a case of chronic rectal prolapse, carefully avoiding presacral veins while following the curve of the coccyx. The levator muscle is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with a right-sided dissection, joining the posterior plane.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree scope for better exposure, attempting to develop a rectovaginal septum but finds the plane obscured due to a previous hysterectomy. The dissection focuses on the posterior and right lateral areas, avoiding further anterior exploration. Silk sutures are used to take bites of the lateral stalk up to the rectal wall without penetrating it.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to a thirty-degree angle for better exposure, revealing a clear surgical plane. Attempts are made to develop a rectovaginal septum, but the previous hysterectomy has obscured the fat plane. The surgeon palpates the area and identifies the perineal body, deciding not to proceed further anteriorly.

The surgeon performs a posterior and right lateral dissection, using silk sutures to take bites of the lateral stalk up to the rectal wall without penetrating it. The dissection is visible as the surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue around the rectal wall.

The surgeon takes careful bites of the periosteum during the rectopexy, ensuring the iliac vessels are clear from the working area. The procedure is completed with two stitches, and the peritoneal opening is closed to prevent small bowel obstruction.

This is an elderly patient with several comorbidities, and they were discharged from the recovery room. The key to these bites, I think, is more the sacral bite, getting a really good bite of that periosteum. Sometimes it's scary taking those bites and the needle comes through and there's bleeding, which is why I think it's important at the start of your dissection to really clear off the periosteum to make sure that there's no venous tributaries there. And certainly like in a case like this, making sure that the iliacs are well away from where you're working. And that completes my pexy.
The surgeon places two stitches to close the peritoneal opening, aiming to prevent small bowel obstruction by avoiding its descent into the opening.

The surgeon carefully navigates the peritoneal fold on the right lateral side, ensuring not to inadvertently grasp the right ureter during the procedure. The video concludes with a view of the completed rectopexy, showing a well-suspended rectum.
The surgeon carefully navigates the peritoneal fold on the right lateral side, ensuring not to inadvertently take a large bite that could involve the right ureter during dissection.
The surgeon displays the final result of the procedure, showing a well-suspended rectum after eliminating the need for sigmoid resections.
